posts.title: Wine Ordering: A Guide for the Confident Host
posts.body:
I consder my self one of the most fortunate and blessed people I know. I truly love my job. Working in risk management comes with a range of perks, one of which is the privilege of dining and sharing great wine with colleagues, clients, and friends. Whether at industry events or intimate dinners, there is something undeniably special about bonding over a great meal and an impressive bottle of wine. Over the years more people than I can count have asked me to put something like this together. So here it is.

## Why Wine

For me wine is not about chasing a buzz. If that is your goal, save yourself the time and money and grab a bottle of Jack. There is a unique romance about wine that no other drink can replicate. It has the power to bring food and people together in a way that is hard to explain until you experience it. Most of you know I am notorious for my four and five hour dinners. Wine is how those happen. It naturally pairs with a meal, enhances the experience, and sparks the kind of conversation you never want to end.

Wine is also wonderfully diverse, much like we are as people. It comes in a variety of styles from different regions and backgrounds, and it evolves over time. A bottle can taste completely different from one day to the next. Even your mood can influence how it lands on the palate. And of course, it simply tastes great.

I would not call myself a wine expert. Well versed is probably the right description. There are plenty of professionals who could run laps around my knowledge. But through the years I have developed some simple strategies for picking and ordering wine at restaurants that can help even the most casual wine drinker feel confident at the table.

## Who Is This Guide For

This is for anyone who dines at restaurants where wine is a featured part of the experience. Anyone who enjoys wine but does not necessarily consider themselves an expert. Anyone who finds themselves as the host at a dinner, whether entertaining clients, celebrating with loved ones, or just being that brave soul at the table willing to pick up the wine list when everyone else looks the other way. And honestly, sometimes it is just nice to impress the people you care about.

## Build Your Personal Wine Journal

Before we even get to the restaurant, start here. Every time you enjoy a wine worth remembering, take note of it. Grab your phone, snap a photo of the label, jot down a few words about what you liked. There are apps built specifically for this but the notes app on your phone works just fine. Here is what to look for:

The grape variety. Is it a Pinot Noir, Cabernet Sauvignon, Chardonnay, or something else? If it is a blend or the label is unclear, check the back label for the breakdown. For foreign wines that are harder to decode, ask your server or just Google it.

Where it is from. Napa, Sonoma, Washington, France, Italy, wherever. If it is not immediately clear, ask or look it up.

The sub-region. Go a level deeper. If it is from Sonoma, is it Alexander Valley or Russian River Valley? If it is Napa, is it Stags Leap, Oakville, or St. Helena? The more detail you have, the more informed your choices become over time.

The vintage. Check the year on the bottle to understand its age.

The price. Most restaurants list it. If not, look it up later. This helps you calibrate quality against cost over time.

The more you practice this the more intuitive it becomes. You are building a memory bank that pays dividends every time you sit down with a wine list.

## The Moment of Truth

You know the scene. Everyone is seated, menus have been handed out, and then the wine list lands on the table. More often than not guests react as if a grenade has been placed in front of them. Some freeze. Some look away. As the host or that brave soul willing to take charge, you pick it up. Now what?

First, gauge the table. Ask directly if everyone plans to drink wine. If two or more people are in, and you expect at least a glass or two each, ordering a bottle is almost always the better choice. Most restaurants offer wine by the glass but the selection is limited and the cost per glass is typically higher than splitting a bottle. A bottle also adds a shared experience that a round of individual glasses just does not replicate.

Second, keep the momentum going while you decide. Suggest cocktails, beer, or a glass of something simple to keep guests satisfied while you study the list. From the moment you receive the wine list to the first glass being poured can easily be ten to twenty minutes. Don't let guests sit empty handed.

Third, discover your group's preferences. Red or white is usually the first question. You will find most tables lean red but some guests have firm preferences. If one or two people insist on something specific let them order by the glass while you focus on a bottle that works for the majority. This is where your wine journal memory bank becomes your secret weapon. Look at the list and recall what you have enjoyed, what regions you loved, what styles resonated with you. If you once fell in love with a Pinot Noir from Oregon's Willamette Valley, look for something from that area. You are using your own experience as a guide, not trying to become an expert overnight.

## A Few More Things Worth Knowing

Choose restaurants known for well-curated wine lists. Fine dining spots are generally a safe bet and the staff will be more equipped to help you navigate.

Pairings are flexible. While matching wine to food perfectly is ideal it is not a hard rule. Red with fish, white with steak, it is all fine as long as the experience is enjoyable. If your table loves red but you are starting with seafood, a lighter red like a Pinot Noir from Oregon or Santa Barbara is a beautiful bridge between both worlds.

Budget wisely. In most fine dining restaurants quality red wines typically range from $100 to $200 and good whites from $50 to $100. You do not need to break the bank to enjoy something special.

Old World (Europe) wines are incredible but can be intimidating if you are not familiar with them. European labels often do not list the grape variety because you are expected to know the region. If you venture there and are not sure, own it. Tell your guests you are living on the wild side and enjoy the ride.

Lean on the experts. If you are stumped, ask the sommelier or a knowledgeable server. Share your past favorites, preferred regions, and budget. If you are concerned about price, simply point to a number on the wine list you are comfortable with rather than saying the amount out loud. It saves the awkwardness and gets you exactly where you need to be.

## Pro Tips

If you arrive early and have the opportunity to sit at the bar, this is your power move. Pick your bottle, have it opened right there, and let it breathe before your guests even sit down. The wine will be ready to drink the moment everyone is seated, you skip the pressure of choosing under the spotlight, and your guests will think it is the most effortless, baller move they have ever seen at a dinner. It absolutely is.

Most restaurants publish their wine list on their website. If you have ten minutes on the Uber ride over, take a quick look. You will arrive with a head start, a shortlist in your mind, and a confidence that the table will notice immediately.

Most restaurant wines are younger than the ideal age they should be consumed at. If you spend $100 or more on a bottle of red and it is not blowing you away on the first pour, do not hesitate to ask the server to have it decanted. Decanting opens the wine up and can transform it completely within twenty to thirty minutes. One thing to keep in mind though, if someone at the table is not having a cocktail and is literally sitting there waiting for the bottle, have a small amount poured for them right away so they have something to sip on while the wine opens up. Taking care of your guests is always the move.

## In Closing

There are very few right or wrong answers when it comes to wine. The process is as personal as it is enjoyable. Knowing how to choose and order a bottle with confidence does not require an encyclopedia of knowledge. It requires curiosity, a little practice, and the willingness to enjoy the journey.

The goal was never to make you an expert overnight. The goal is to make sure that the next time that wine list lands on the table, you pick it up with confidence, take care of your people, and let the evening do the rest.

Cheers. And as always, Journey Wine With Us. 🍷
